response:
The fare calculation begins with a base fee of ten yuan for the first four kilometers of any journey. When the distance exceeds this initial limit the driver charges an extra two yuan for every additional kilometer traveled. This pricing model ensures that passengers pay more as their trip extends further into the city during spring. The total cost depends directly on the rounded up distance value which includes any converted waiting time. A spring breeze might blow through the streets while the meter ticks up steadily for the passenger.

If the final bill was thirty eight yuan then the total calculated distance must have been twenty three kilometers. Since the actual road distance was fifteen kilometers the difference of eight kilometers represents the converted waiting time. Each five minutes of stopping time counts as one kilometer so the driver waited for forty minutes at most. This maximum stop duration explains how the fare increased significantly beyond the standard rate for the route. The passenger likely experienced a long wait in the vehicle while the driver dealt with traffic or other delays. Such a scenario highlights how time can become money in the taxi industry during the busy spring season. The math confirms that forty minutes is the upper limit for the stops given the final payment amount.
